text.title: systemd User Services: Patterns and Operations
text.summary: Linger for boot persistence, drop-in overrides, timer units for scheduled tasks, essential commands, and troubleshooting table.

rule.statement: Run `loginctl enable-linger` once per user account. Without it, user services stop when the last login session ends and do not start at boot.
rule.rationale: By default, the user's systemd manager exits when the last login session (SSH, TTY, sudo) ends. Linger keeps a permanent user session alive from boot to shutdown, regardless of login state — identical to how system services work, but without root.

example.code:
# Enable (run as root, or the user themselves) loginctl enable-linger nero
# Verify loginctl show-user nero | grep Linger # Linger=yes
# After enabling, enable and start services normally systemctl --user daemon-reload systemctl --user enable nero-core nero-channel-web nero-channel-tg systemctl --user start nero-core nero-channel-web nero-channel-tg

rule.statement: Use `systemctl --user edit name.service` to create drop-in overrides rather than modifying the unit file directly. Drop-ins survive unit file replacements during deploy.
rule.rationale: If the deploy script overwrites the unit file, any manual edits are lost. A drop-in file (`~/.config/systemd/user/nero-core.service.d/override.conf`) is merged at runtime and is not affected by rewriting the base unit.

example.code:
# Creates ~/.config/systemd/user/nero-core.service.d/override.conf systemctl --user edit nero-core
# File created by the editor: [Service] MemoryMax=16G # temporarily increase for a heavy task

rule.statement: Use systemd timer units instead of cron for scheduled tasks. Timers log to journald, support `Persistent=true` (catch up after downtime), and can be managed with `systemctl --user`.
rule.rationale: cron sends output to email (usually lost) and silently misses runs after reboots. `Persistent=true` on a timer runs the missed execution once after boot if the scheduled time was missed. `journalctl --user -u backup.service` gives the full run history with timestamps.

example.code:
# ~/.config/systemd/user/backup.timer [Unit] Description=Daily backup timer
[Timer] OnCalendar=daily Persistent=true RandomizedDelaySec=300
[Install] WantedBy=timers.target

rule.statement: Create a `.target` unit to group related services. `systemctl --user start nero.target` then starts all services in the group in the correct order.
rule.rationale: Starting and stopping a multi-service platform one service at a time is error-prone. A target unit encodes the group membership once; operators (and deploy scripts) use a single command.

example.code:
# ~/.config/systemd/user/nero.target [Unit] Description=NERO Platform Wants=nero-core.service nero-channel-web.service nero-channel-tg.service nero-web.service
[Install] WantedBy=default.target

rule.statement: When a service fails to start, check `journalctl --user -u name -n 50 --no-pager` first, then `systemctl --user status name`. Most startup failures are captured in the last 10 log lines.
rule.rationale: The status output truncates logs. The `journalctl` command shows the full stderr/stdout from ExecStart, including Python tracebacks and missing module errors that are cut off in the status summary.

example.code: Problem Cause Fix Service stops on logout Linger not enabled loginctl enable-linger "Failed to connect to bus" No DBUS in session Export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR Permission denied port <1024 User can't bind low port Use nginx reverse proxy ExecStart fails silently Relative path Use absolute path in ExecStart Secrets visible in show Inline Environment= Use EnvironmentFile= instead Restart loop (failed) StartLimitBurst reached Fix root cause; reset-failed OOM killed MemoryMax too low Increase limit or tune app

# Reset a service stuck in "failed" state after fixing the root cause systemctl --user reset-failed nero-core systemctl --user start nero-core
# Check if linger is enabled loginctl show-user nero | grep Linger
# Watch all user service logs combined journalctl --user -f

see-also.link: Unit file structure and resource limits
